title: Quick check, SDP and codec negotiation

questions.prompt: You read a 200 OK SDP body and see `c=IN IP4 192.168.1.50` and `m=audio 18000 RTP/AVP 8`. The calling endpoint is on the public Internet. What's the most likely consequence?
choices: Codec mismatch; call will fail with 488 Not Acceptable Here
The caller's RTP will be sent to 192.168.1.50 (a private address) and never arrive; classic one-way or no-audio symptom
The call will use the wrong sample rate
DTMF won't work but audio will be fine
questions.explanation: The `c=` line names the IP where RTP should be sent for this leg. A private RFC 1918 address that's not reachable from the caller's side means the caller's outbound RTP goes nowhere. SIP succeeds, audio fails. The fix is on the answerer side: NAT handling on the SIP profile so the PBX rewrites SDP correctly.

questions.prompt: An INVITE offers payload types `0 8 9 96 101` with rtpmap lines mapping them to PCMU, PCMA, G722, Opus, and telephone-event. The 200 OK answer contains `m=audio 18000 RTP/AVP 8 101` only. What does that mean?
choices: The answerer rejected the call; 488 will follow
The answerer picked PCMA as the audio codec and kept telephone-event for RFC 4733 DTMF
The answerer chose all five codecs and will rotate between them
The answer is malformed; the m-line must list all offered codecs
questions.explanation: RFC 3264 offer/answer: the answerer picks ONE audio codec from the offer (here, PCMA / payload type 8) plus the telephone-event entry for DTMF. The answer SDP lists only what was selected. RTP for this call will use PCMA in both directions.
